name: Crete Extended
description: Crete, a large island in the Aegean Sea, held immense significance in Greek mythology. It was the birthplace of Zeus, hidden in the Dikteon Cave to protect him from his father Cronus. Knossos, the island's capital, was home to the labyrinth built by Daedalus for King Minos to imprison the Minotaur, a creature half-man, half-bull. Theseus, aided by Ariadne, navigated the labyrinth and slew the Minotaur, marking a pivotal moment in Greek heroism. The island was also associated with Europa, who was abducted by Zeus in the form of a bull and brought to Crete, where she gave birth to Minos. Cretan culture and its bull-leaping rituals were integral to the development of the Minoan civilization, which heavily influenced Mycenaean Greece. The island's fertile land and strategic location made it a center of power and a stage for many legendary events, solidifying its place as a cornerstone of Greek mythology and history. Mount Ida, the highest mountain in Crete, was another sacred site, revered as the place where Rhea hid Zeus. The island's connection to powerful gods, mythical creatures, and pivotal heroic journeys cemented its importance in the Greek world, both real and imagined. The Gortyn Code, an ancient law code, also lends historical depth to the island's significance. The Minoan civilization, flourishing on Crete, was a maritime power that traded across the Mediterranean, further enriching its cultural and mythical landscape. The island's landscape, from its fertile plains to its rugged mountains, became a canvas for the unfolding dramas of gods and heroes.
subtitle: Island of Gods, Heroes, and Labyrinths

keyMyths.title: The Birth of Zeus
keyMyths.description: Rhea hid the newborn Zeus in the Dikteon Cave on Crete to protect him from his father, Cronus, who devoured his children. He was raised by nymphs and fed by the goat Amalthea.

keyMyths.title: Theseus and the Minotaur
keyMyths.description: Theseus journeyed to Crete to slay the Minotaur in the labyrinth. With Ariadne's help, he navigated the maze and killed the beast, freeing Athens from its tribute.

sources.author: Hesiod
sources.description: A genealogical account of the gods.
